text: We are interested in measuring the levels of cellular metabolites using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) in a very high throughput fashion. Metabolites are identified by their mass spectrums and the chromatographic retention time. Recently with the advent of rapid scanning high resolution and high accuracy mass spectrometers, many people are trying to get by with as little as possible chromatography, particularly with source such as ESI which is a soft ionization method. Although chromatography may not be necessary or desired for the analysis of pure compounds, we have found that the complex cellular extracts that we have for LC-MS analysis require some form of chromatography in order for the compounds to be accurately measured, and that the lack of chromatography can lead to the mis-annotation of structurally related compounds (i.e. the in-source fragmentation products of a compound will have the same mass as the compound, and conversely, the parent compound will have the same mass as its fragmentation products). For example nucleotide-triphosphates fragment into nucleotide-diphosphates and the hexose-phosphates fragment into triose-phosphates. We have measured the yeast intracellular metabolites, and there are over 20 cases of in which the in-source fragments are of metabolites that are of major interest. In order to measure the metabolites of interest accurately in our experiments, chromatography must be used.
explanation: Technical and domain-specific content with plausible experimental details; it reads like human-written methods text. No obvious AI-generated artifacts detected.
